major.message: The workflow-test step runs 4 node --test files sequentially in a single run: block. GitHub Actions executes run: blocks with bash -eo pipefail by default, so if ai-sdlc-gate.test.mjs fails, the remaining 3 test files never execute. On a red-main event caused by a dor-ingress or fork-pr-safety test failure, the step exits with the first failure's output and the other test results are silently missing. Diagnostic coverage degrades to whichever test happens to be first. Fix: run each test file as a separate step (each with continue-on-error: true and its own id), then aggregate outcomes in the Determine test outcome step -- the same pattern used for full-test vs workflow-test already.

minor.message: The BODY variable is assigned via an indented multi-line double-quoted string. Every line inside the quotes inherits the ~10-space YAML indentation from the run: block, so the created GitHub issue will have leading spaces on every line. In GitHub's markdown renderer, 4+ leading spaces trigger a code-block interpretation, meaning the entire issue body may render as a single preformatted block rather than formatted markdown. Fix: use a heredoc with printf or a dedented assignment.

gh issue create uses hardcoded --label "ci,main-red". If either label does not exist in the repository, gh will exit non-zero and no issue will be filed -- the alert job will fail, but the failure reason (missing label, not test failure) will be confusing and the on-call operator won't get paged. The workflow should either pre-create the labels or use --label only if the labels are known to exist. Consider removing --label from the create command and adding it in a follow-up step, or document that these labels must exist as a setup prerequisite.

The duplicate-issue check uses --search "in:title [main-health] main is RED" which is a substring match; two concurrent pushes can both find EXISTING=0 before either creates an issue, resulting in duplicate alert issues. The code acknowledges this race with a comment. Given the concurrency group serializes runs (cancel-in-progress: false), true concurrent execution is impossible for the same workflow -- but the race can occur between two different pushes if the first workflow's alert job hasn't finished creating the issue by the time the second workflow's alert job runs its check. This is low-probability but worth noting.

suggestion.message: No hermetic test exists for main-health-monitor.yml. The repo has a clear pattern of structural workflow tests (dor-ingress.test.mjs, ai-sdlc-gate.test.mjs, fork-pr-safety.test.mjs, etc.) for workflows with load-bearing conditions. This workflow has four conditions that are worth locking structurally: (1) trigger is push:branches:[main] only — no pull_request trigger — so a future accidental PR trigger would turn this post-merge health check into a pre-merge gate; (2) concurrency.cancel-in-progress: false — inverting this would silently drop in-flight health checks when a fix lands on main; (3) the alert job's `if: failure()` condition — if this were changed to `if: always()`, issues would be filed on every successful push; (4) both test steps carry continue-on-error: true — removing this would cause the health-check job to exit before capturing the combined outcome. A test file at .github/workflows/__tests__/main-health-monitor.test.mjs using the same python3 YAML-parse + node:test pattern would take ~30 lines and protect these invariants. This is a suggestion, not a blocker, because CI execution validates the workflow and the conditions are well-documented inline.

Defense-in-depth (not a vulnerability): `COMMIT_MSG` (`github.event.head_commit.message`) is correctly passed via `env:` and expanded as a shell variable `${COMMIT_MSG}` inside the issue BODY — this is the safe pattern and prevents expression injection. The only residual effect is that a crafted commit message could inject markdown into the filed issue body (cosmetic spoofing). Since this fires only on push to main (post-merge, gated code) and the value is never executed, no action is required. If you ever want to harden further, fence the message in a code block.
